title: Pork pie with hot water crust
subtitle: A free-standing raised pie of seasoned pork in crisp hot water crust pastry
excerpt: A traditional pork pie uses hot water crust pastry, which is made with boiling water and lard, and is firm enough to stand without a tin. The filling is seasoned pork shoulder and belly, pressed in, and the pie is baked until the pastry is deep amber and the meat is cooked through. Stock is poured in through the lid vent after baking, which sets to a jelly as the pie cools.
sourceNotes: Raised hot water crust pork pies are a centuries-old British tradition, particularly associated with Melton Mowbray and Yorkshire. The hot water crust method is documented in British domestic cookery from the seventeenth century onward. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg, gives the standard method. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents regional variants.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pie must be made at least one day ahead; the jelly needs overnight to set. Keeps in the fridge for 4 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; start high to set the crust, drop to 180°C after 20 minutes

content.text: A pork pie is built from hot water crust pastry, which is made by melting lard into boiling water and beating in flour while the mixture is still hot. The resulting pastry is malleable and stiff, nothing like a short pastry, and is pressed into shape rather than rolled. It holds without a tin. The filling is raw pork seasoned with salt, pepper, and mace; the jelly poured in after baking is what makes the pie work structurally when cold.

items.prepNote: good pork stock or chicken stock; for the jelly

content.text: Bring the lard and water to a full boil in a saucepan. Pour into the flour and salt and beat vigorously with a spoon until a smooth dough forms. Turn out and knead briefly until smooth. Work quickly while the pastry is still warm and pliable; it stiffens as it cools. Set aside a quarter of the dough for the lid. Press the remainder into a greased 20 cm springform tin, working it evenly up the sides to a height of about 7 cm.

Combine the pork shoulder, pork belly, salt, white pepper, and mace in a bowl. Mix well; the seasoning must be generous or the pie will be bland when cold. Pack the filling firmly into the pastry case, pressing out any air pockets. Roll the reserved pastry into a circle for the lid. Press onto the top, seal firmly, and crimp the edge. Cut a central steam vent of about 1 cm diameter.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush the lid with beaten egg. Bake for 20 minutes, then reduce to 180°C and bake for a further 70 minutes. The pie is done when the pastry is deep amber and a probe through the central vent reads 75°C in the centre of the filling. Leave to cool at room temperature for 1 hour.
Fill with jelly and chill overnight

Heat the stock until hot. Squeeze out the gelatine leaves and stir into the stock until dissolved. Pour slowly through the central vent in the lid, pausing to let it settle. Fill until you cannot add any more without overflow. The jelly fills the gaps left by meat contraction during baking. Refrigerate overnight. Remove from the tin to slice; the pastry should be firm and free-standing.
